_description: Security exceptions for the CI self-check gate. This file suppresses false positives that arise when the security-mcp tool scans its own source code. It is NOT used for user project gate runs.

exceptions.justification: security-mcp is a developer CLI security scanner tool, not an application server. Controls requiring auth middleware, MFA enforcement, audit logging infrastructure, egress controls, and network segmentation are not applicable to this tool's architecture. The tool itself enforces these controls in user project code — it does not implement them internally.

SLSA provenance is generated by GitHub Actions via 'npm publish --provenance' and is attached to the npm package registry entry. The tool does not produce .intoto.jsonl files in .mcp/attestations/ because provenance is managed at the npm publish layer, not at the repo layer.

These findings come from the REMEDIATION_MAP in src/mcp/server.ts, which contains intentional 'before' example code patterns (e.g., sslmode=disable, md5 hashing, console.log(req.body)) used to show developers how to fix vulnerabilities. The patterns appear in string literals as educational examples, not as actual application code. WEB_IDOR_RISK matches a db.query example line in the remediation map (not a real DB query); WEB_LOG_PII matches a console.log fix-example and a grep pattern inside SKILL.md educational content; WEB_SESSION_WEAK_CONFIG matches cookie-config examples inside SKILL.md files that teach session hardening. security-mcp has no HTTP server, no user sessions, and no cookies — these checks do not apply to its architecture. This is a known false-positive class for security tools that document their own remediation guidance. Also covers GraphQL patterns found only in documentation and checklists (README.md, SKILL.md), not in a real GraphQL server.

Runtime dependencies (execa, fast-glob, picomatch, zod) have low OpenSSF Scorecard scores due to minimal OSSF project infrastructure (no Scorecard workflow, branch protection). These are mature, widely-used packages with no known critical CVEs. Replacement would introduce more risk than retention. Risk accepted pending upstream scorecard improvements.

The CI self-check environment has an empty scanner config (.github/security-tools-ci.json) because external scanners (gitleaks, semgrep, osv-scanner) are not installed in the GitHub Actions runner for this self-check step. The tool's own built-in checks cover the equivalent detections. Users are expected to have these tools installed in their own environments.

False positives from patterns matched in non-application files: DANGEROUSLY_SET_INNER_HTML matched in .mcp/baselines/ JSON data (a stored scan result, not code); SSRF_GUARD_REQUIRED matched 'undici-types' in package-lock.json (a type-definition package, not an HTTP client usage); IAM_OVERPRIVILEGED matched 'Action: *' in README.md documentation showing an anti-pattern; PUBLIC_EXPOSURE_RISK matched '0.0.0.0/0' in README.md explaining what to avoid; JWT_ALG_NONE_ACCEPTED matched a checklist item in server.ts that documents JWT security requirements as a string literal, not actual jwt.verify() code; WEB_HEADERS_MISSING and SSRF_USER_URL matched HTTP calls in src/cli/ that have explicit hostname allowlist checks (ALLOWED_SKILL_HOSTS, ALLOWED_BINARY_HOSTS) validating URLs before every fetch — all callers pass only hardcoded constants or pre-allowlisted URLs. security-mcp is a CLI/MCP scanner tool with no HTTP server surface, no JWT auth, and no IAM policies.
